question: Which of the following sets is countable?
options: The set of all integers, Z
The set of all real numbers, R
The set of all irrational numbers
The set of all subsets of the natural numbers, P(N)
explanation: The set of integers Z is countable because its elements can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers N (e.g., 0, 1, -1, 2, -2, ...).

question: What is the cardinality of the set of rational numbers, Q?
options: ℵ₀ (aleph-null)
c (the cardinality of the continuum)
Uncountable but less than c
It is finite
explanation: The set of rational numbers Q is countable (has cardinality ℵ₀). This can be proven by arranging the rationals in a grid and using a diagonal counting argument to list them all.

question: Cantor's diagonalization argument is primarily used to prove that:
options: The real numbers between 0 and 1 are uncountable
The set of integers is countable
The union of two countable sets is countable
Every infinite set has a countable subset
explanation: Cantor's diagonal argument constructs a real number not on any given list, proving that the interval (0,1) and thus the entire set of real numbers R is uncountable.

question: Which of the following statements about the set of all algebraic numbers is TRUE?
options: It is countable.
It is uncountable and has the same cardinality as R.
It is a proper subset of the rational numbers, Q.
Its cardinality is strictly greater than that of the real numbers, R.
explanation: An algebraic number is a root of a non-zero polynomial with integer coefficients. Since there are countably many such polynomials and each has a finite number of roots, the set of all algebraic numbers is countable.

question: According to the Continuum Hypothesis, which of the following is believed to be true?
options: There is no set whose cardinality is strictly between |N| and |R|.
The cardinality of R is ℵ₁.
Every infinite set of real numbers is uncountable.
The set of all functions from R to R has the same cardinality as R.
explanation: The Continuum Hypothesis states that there is no set with cardinality strictly between that of the natural numbers (ℵ₀) and the real numbers (c). It is independent of the standard ZFC axioms of set theory.
